title: Listening - Part 4: Making Ceramics by the Coast
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Maya, a ceramic artist. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m talking to Maya Brooks, who makes ceramic bowls and cups by the coast. Maya, you used to work in an office, didn’t you? || Maya: Yes, in accounts. It was a secure job, so leaving was not obvious. I began taking evening pottery classes after particularly stressful days. [24]What drew me in was that shaping clay required all my attention, so for a couple of hours I stopped thinking about emails and figures[/24]. Eventually I realised I wanted that concentration to be central to my working life. || Presenter: Was making things professionally much harder than attending a class? || Maya: Considerably. In class I made one bowl at a time and felt proud if it looked attractive. Once cafés began ordering from me, I discovered that [25]producing twenty cups which were the same size was far more demanding than making one unusual piece[/25]. Cups must stack properly. || Presenter: Your pots have a particular colour. How do you achieve that? || Maya: I mix a little local clay, collected with permission from a building site, into the glaze. [26]I use it because the warm, sandy colour reflects the landscape where the pots are made[/26]. Preparing it takes time. || Presenter: Has anything gone wrong in the studio? || Maya: Many things! Early on, I had a large restaurant order and was worried about finishing on time. I put the pieces in the kiln before they had dried fully. [27]Several developed cracks during firing because I had tried to hurry the drying stage[/27]. I had to remake the order, and I now plan extra time for every job. || Presenter: Does selling online help an artist like you? || Maya: It helps people discover the studio. A short video of a mug being made can bring visitors from miles away. However, [28]online photographs cannot show a customer the weight and feel of a cup in their hands[/28]. That is why I still enjoy selling at craft markets, where people can pick things up before choosing. || Presenter: You also run weekend workshops. What do participants get from them? || Maya: Most arrive saying they have no artistic ability. Their first pot is rarely perfectly round, but that becomes less important as the day goes on. [29]The best moment is when people begin to enjoy the marks their own fingers have left in the clay[/29]. They realise handmade work does not have to look machine-made. || Presenter: What would you like to do next? || Maya: Firing a kiln uses a lot of electricity. I’m discussing a project with three other potters. [30]We hope to share one larger kiln and coordinate firings so that we use less energy[/30]. It would also give us more chances to exchange advice, rather than each of us working alone.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Maya initially enjoy pottery classes?

options.A: They helped her forget work pressures.
options.B: They offered a way to earn extra money.
options.C: They introduced her to new colleagues.

questions.explanation: Maya says shaping clay demanded her attention and stopped her thinking about office work.

questions.question: What surprised Maya about becoming a professional potter?
questions.explanation: She found producing twenty cups of the same size harder than making one original item.

questions.question: Why does Maya include local clay in her glaze?

options.B: It costs less than imported clay.
options.C: It gives her work a local character.

questions.explanation: The warm colour reflects the landscape where her pots are made.

questions.question: Why did some pots crack during an early order?

options.A: She fired them before they were dry enough.
options.B: She used the wrong type of clay.
options.C: She removed them from the kiln too soon.

questions.explanation: Maya says she hurried the drying stage before firing the pieces.

questions.question: What limitation does Maya see in selling online?

options.B: Videos take too long to produce.
options.C: Customers cannot experience the object physically.

questions.explanation: Photographs cannot show the weight and feel of a cup.

questions.question: What does Maya particularly value in her workshops?

options.A: Participants accepting the individual character of their work.
options.B: Participants producing items they can sell.
options.C: Participants learning to use professional equipment.

questions.explanation: She enjoys when people appreciate the finger marks in their handmade pots.

questions.question: What does Maya plan to do with other potters?

options.B: Reduce energy use by sharing equipment.
options.C: Create a single range of products.

questions.explanation: They plan to share a larger kiln and coordinate firings to use less energy.
